one_line_takeaway: QRS flips the typical LLM-as-SAST-triage pattern by having ensembles of LLM agents author, review, and sanitize their own CodeQL queries end-to-end, finding 5 new CVEs and rediscovering 29 known ones across the top 100 PyPI packages (vs. 0 novel CVEs for CodeQL/Opengrep/Bandit on the same set) at the cost of materially lower precision (22-49%) than baseline LLM vulnerability-detection literature and a nontrivial per-scan API bill.
key_finding: On Hist20, the four-configuration consensus detected 19/20 known CVEs (average per-config consensus 2.7/4); best single config DR0 (DeepSeek-Reasoner) reached 90.62% accuracy / 86.96% precision / 100% recall (F1 0.930); C0 (Claude) got 86.44% accuracy but only 75.76% precision/recall. On Top100 (1000 scans), QRS surfaced 39 medium-to-high severity vulnerabilities total: 29 rediscoveries of previously known but undisclosed-to-the-tool vulnerable code paths, 5 newly disclosed and CVE-assigned issues (CVE-2025-68146 and CVE-2026-22701 TOCTOU race conditions in filelock, CVE-2026-22702 chained race condition in virtualenv, CVE-2026-23949 zip-slip path traversal in jaraco.context/setuptools CVSS 8.6, CVE-2026-23490 ASN.1 REL-OID unbounded-recursion memory exhaustion in pyasn1), and 5 maintainer-acknowledged non-CVE issues (numpy f2py path traversal and code execution, jsonschema ReDoS, s3transfer weak-RNG TOCTOU, aiohttp CookieJar pickle deserialization). Against the same Top100 corpus, on the 39 ground-truth vulnerabilities: QRS detected 39/39 (100%), Bandit 11/39 (28.2%), Opengrep 10/39 (25.6%), CodeQL 0/39 (0%); alert volumes were Opengrep 1251 warnings, Bandit 653 medium/high alerts, CodeQL 136 findings, versus QRS's noisiest configuration (C0) producing 140 TP candidate findings after S-agent filtering (a 64.8% reduction from R agent's 398 raw TP candidates). Top100 aggregate performance for C0: 93.50% accuracy, 44.23% precision, 92.00% recall, F1 0.597 (precision is consistently the weak point across all 10 configs in the open-world setting, ranging ~22-49%, while recall stays >=85% in nearly every config and hits 100% for several). False-negative rate: 2.2% (9/406) on Hist20, 0.3% (14/3748) on Top100. Total experimental cost across all runs: 203h45m runtime, $543.34, 332.8M tokens, 30,646 iterations; average per-scan cost ranged from $0.06 (DeepSeek-Chat) to $1.45 (Claude C0), with DeepSeek-Reasoner offering the best accuracy/cost tradeoff on Hist20 (~$0.08/scan) while Claude dominated real-world Top100 discovery (found 'almost all' 39 vulnerabilities) despite highest cost. One known CVE (CVE-2021-32677, FastAPI open redirect) was missed by every configuration; the paper attributes this to reasoning about HTTP Content-Type-conditioned CORS-preflight exemption semantics not captured by the current QL knowledge schema.
core_contribution: QRS (Query, Review, Sanitize) is a neuro-symbolic multi-agent framework that inverts the usual LLM+SAST pipeline: instead of using an LLM to triage/filter existing static-analysis tool outputs, QRS uses LLMs as the generative core to author CodeQL queries from a compact hand-written schema (knowledge.md) and few-shot examples. Three autonomous agents operate in sequence: (1) the Q (Query) agent profiles the target package once, then iteratively synthesizes and self-heals CodeQL queries (bounded 3-attempt fixup_query retry loop) from high-level vulnerability hypotheses, tagging each with a CWE and severity; (2) the R (Review) agent clusters raw findings by MITRE CWE taxonomy, reconstructs code context via trace_flow (source-to-sink data-flow slicing) and grep_search, renders an evidence-based verdict (TP >=90% confidence / Manual Review >=70% / FP), and synthesizes a working proof-of-concept exploit for each accepted finding; (3) the S (Sanitize) agent re-reviews findings context-free/evidence-only, checks a CVE database (search_cve) to label KNOWN_CVE vs ZERO_DAY vs NOVEL_FINDING, screens for test/build code and other false-positive sources, and applies a multi-label taxonomy (including a HALLUCINATION flag). The framework is model-agnostic via LiteLLM, tested across five model families (Claude Sonnet 4.5/Haiku 4.5, DeepSeek Chat, DeepSeek Reasoner, Gemini 3 Pro/2.5 Pro, GPT-5.1/4.1) at two temperatures (0 and 1), giving 10 configurations. All outputs go through mandatory manual expert review before final acceptance.
threat_model: Defenders have full source-code access to a package and want to find exploitable vulnerabilities before attackers do, using LLM assistance to author CodeQL queries; CodeQL execution is local and the LLM sees only tool output (CodeQL query text/results), not the full codebase. The paper explicitly scopes out: vulnerabilities in compiled extensions/native code, logic bugs needing dynamic analysis, distribution-level supply-chain attacks (e.g., compromised PyPI uploads), and adversarial evasion where an attacker deliberately crafts code to defeat QRS's LLM-guided query synthesis (stated as future work, not evaluated).
limitations: Authors' own stated/observed limitations: (1) Hist20 sample size is only N=20, explicitly flagged as requiring cautious interpretation; (2) ground truth for Top100 relies on expert review plus maintainer response rather than an exhaustive manual audit, which the authors say could underestimate both TP and FP rates, and introduces 'unavoidable subjectivity'; (3) precision is the framework's dominant weakness (best precision only 48.5% in the Top100/LLM-comparison table), by explicit design choice favoring recall since missed vulnerabilities are considered costlier than false positives in a supply-chain-scanning context; (4) evaluated only on Python packages (via CodeQL's Python support) — cross-language extension is described as straightforward in principle but not implemented or tested; (5) adversarial evasion of the LLM-guided query synthesis (attacker-crafted code intended to defeat QRS) is explicitly out of scope and unevaluated; (6) native/compiled extensions and logic bugs requiring dynamic analysis are excluded from scope; (7) individual model behavior varies substantially and unpredictably — GPT models terminated prematurely and had the highest query-compilation failure rates (59-68%), DeepSeek-Chat frequently fell into no-op iterations yielding ~1 finding per scan, and reliable detection required a minimum of ~3-4 overlapping configurations (consensus), meaning a single-model deployment likely underperforms the reported ensemble numbers; (8) total cost of $543.34 for the full experimental sweep, and per-scan cost up to $1.45 (Claude), is nontrivial at scale even though the paper frames it as cheap relative to manual audits; (9) the framework depends on a hand-authored knowledge.md schema and few-shot examples, so its coverage is still bounded by what the authors chose to encode (illustrated by the missed FastAPI open-redirect CVE, attributed to a knowledge-schema gap around HTTP routing semantics).

datasets_used: Hist20: 20 PyPI package versions with 20 disclosed CVEs (2018-2025) across 12 CWE categories, curated by the authors
Top100: the 100 most-downloaded PyPI packages as of November 2025 (per the 'Top PyPI Packages' open-source project snapshot), 144M-1.4B monthly downloads
quantitative_results: Hist20 consensus (4 configs): 19/20 CVEs detected, average consensus 2.7/4
Hist20 DR0 (DeepSeek-Reasoner, temp0): 90.62% accuracy, 86.96% precision, 100.00% recall, F1 0.930
Hist20 C0 (Claude, temp0): 86.44% accuracy, 75.76% precision, 75.76% recall, F1 0.758
Hist20 G0 (Gemini, temp0): 85.07% accuracy, 69.70% precision, 100.00% recall, F1 0.821
Hist20 GPT0 (GPT, temp0): 74.19% accuracy, 44.12% precision, 75.00% recall, F1 0.556
Top100 total findings: 39 (29 known-CVE rediscoveries + 5 newly assigned CVEs + 5 maintainer acknowledgements)
Top100 detection rate vs. baselines on the 39 ground-truth vulnerabilities: QRS 39/39 (100%), Bandit 11/39 (28.21%), Opengrep 10/39 (25.64%), CodeQL 0/39 (0.00%)

Top100 alert volume: Opengrep 1251 warnings, Bandit 653 medium/high-severity alerts (>48k incl. low severity), CodeQL 136 findings, vs. QRS noisiest config (C0) 140 TP findings after S-agent filtering
Top100 C0 (Claude, temp0): 93.50% accuracy, 44.23% precision, 92.00% recall, F1 0.597
Top100 C1 (Claude, temp1): 93.84% accuracy, 43.96% precision, 85.11% recall, F1 0.580
Top100 R-to-S false-positive reduction: C0 398->140 (64.8% reduction), C1 300->103 (65.7%)
Total experimental cost: 203h45m runtime, $543.34, 332.8M tokens, 30,646 agent iterations across the full sweep
Per-scan average cost range: $0.061 (DeepSeek-Chat, temp0) to $1.453 (Claude, temp0)

5 newly disclosed CVEs: CVE-2025-68146 (filelock, CVSS 6.3), CVE-2026-22701 (filelock, CVSS 5.3), CVE-2026-22702 (virtualenv, CVSS 4.5), CVE-2026-23949 (setuptools/jaraco.context zip-slip, CVSS 8.6), CVE-2026-23490 (pyasn1 ASN.1 memory exhaustion, CVSS 7.5)
baselines_compared: Opengrep v1.15.1 (Semgrep fork, standard community Python ruleset)
CodeQL v2.23.0 (GitHub's own standard Python security query suite)

Literature comparison (not run by the authors, taken from prior papers): VulSim, VulBERTa-CNN, ReGVD (pattern-based classifiers); LLMxCPG, LARA (LLM-guided static analysis); LLM4Vuln, VulAgent (multi-agent frameworks)
